text:
DEPARTMENT OF RADIOLOGY
NEUROIMAGING / BODY IMAGING

EXAMINATION: MRI Brain with and without gadolinium contrast; CT Chest with intravenous contrast (concurrent, same-day)

INDICATION: 64-year-old woman with known Stage IIIB non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, right upper lobe, EGFR L858R-mutant, on osi (Tagrisso) therapy for approximately 14 months. Presenting with 3-week history of progressive bifrontal headaches, worse in the morning, and approximately 2 weeks of mild word-finding difficulties. No reported seizures, focal weakness, or visual disturbances. Evaluate for intracranial metastatic disease. Prior MRI brain obtained at initial staging (14 months ago) was negative for intracranial lesions.

TECHNIQUE:
MRI Brain: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the brain was performed on a 3-Tesla magnet including: sagittal T1-weighted localizer, axial T2-weighted fast spin echo, axial FLAIR (fluid-attenuated inversion recovery), axial and coronal T1-weighted pre- and post-gadolinium sequences with fat saturation, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, and susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI). Intravenous gadolinium contrast (gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg) administered without complication.

CT Chest: Axial CT of the chest was acquired following intravenous iodinated contrast administration (nonionic, 80 mL at 3 mL/sec, portal venous phase). Reformats in coronal and sagittal planes were obtained. Prior CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis from 2 months ago is available for comparison (12-month restaging scan).

---

FINDINGS:

MRI BRAIN:

Parenchyma:
Three discrete foci of abnormal enhancement are identified, new in comparison to the prior MRI brain from the time of initial staging:

1. Left frontal lobe: A rounded, well-circumscribed enhancing lesion measuring approximately 12 mm in greatest axial dimension is identified in the left frontal lobe white matter, at the gray-white junction, posterior to the precentral gyrus. The lesion demonstrates avid, homogeneous ring-like enhancement following gadolinium administration with a thin, uniform enhancing rim and a central non-enhancing core. There is moderate surrounding vasogenic edema extending into the adjacent white matter, manifesting as FLAIR signal hyperintensity tracking along the corona radiata for approximately 2 to 2.5 cm. Mild focal mass effect is noted upon the adjacent posterior frontal cortex. No midline shift is present. On DWI, no restricted diffusion within the lesion itself, though the perilesional edema demonstrates elevated signal on b1000 consistent with T2 shine-through. SWI demonstrates no intralesional hemorrhagic products.

2. Right cerebellar hemisphere: A second well-defined enhancing lesion measuring approximately 8 mm in greatest dimension is identified in the right cerebellar hemisphere, subcortically. Enhancement is nodular and homogeneous. There is no significant surrounding vasogenic edema. No mass effect upon the fourth ventricle. No restricted diffusion. No susceptibility signal to suggest hemorrhage.

3. Left parietal lobe: A third small focus of punctate enhancement is identified in the left parietal lobe white matter, measuring approximately 6 mm. Enhancement is nodular and homogeneous. Minimal surrounding FLAIR signal abnormality, not meeting threshold for significant vasogenic edema. No mass effect. No restricted diffusion.

No additional enhancing lesions are identified. Careful review of the brainstem, basal ganglia, thalami, and temporal lobes demonstrates no further suspicious foci. A dedicated thin-slice high-resolution post-contrast sequence (1 mm isotropic) was reviewed.

Leptomeningeal Spaces and Ventricular System:
No abnormal leptomeningeal or pachymeningeal enhancement is identified. The ventricular system is normal in caliber. No hydrocephalus. The fourth ventricle is patent and normally positioned. Basal cisterns are open.

Calvarium and Skull Base:
No calvarial or skull base lesions identified.

Extra-axial Spaces:
No subdural or epidural collections. No subarachnoid hemorrhage.

Background Changes:
Small scattered T2/FLAIR white matter hyperintensities in the periventricular and subcortical regions are again noted, unchanged from the prior study and consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease, appropriate for the patient's age. No new white matter changes.

Pituitary and Orbits:
Pituitary gland is normal in size and configuration. No sellar or parasellar lesion. Orbits are unremarkable.

---

CT CHEST WITH CONTRAST:

Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast obtained approximately 2 months prior (12-month restaging scan), at which time the right upper lobe residual mass measured 1.8 x 1.5 cm and no pathologic adenopathy was identified.

Pulmonary Parenchyma:
Right upper lobe: The previously described residual right upper lobe mass has increased in size compared to the 2-month prior study. The lesion now measures 2.7 cm in its longest dimension (previously 1.8 cm), representing a 50% increase in the longest dimension over the 2-month interval. Morphologically, the mass appears slightly more lobulated at its margins compared to prior, with partial abutment of the posterior parietal pleural surface. No new cavitation or internal air bronchograms are identified. On prior scans, the lesion had trended from 5.8 cm at diagnosis to 3.1 cm at 3 months, 2.0 cm at 6 months, and 1.8 cm at 12 months on Tagrisso; this interval increase is therefore unambiguous and represents clear disease progression after a protracted period of response.

No new pulmonary nodules or masses in the right or left lung. No new consolidation, airspace disease, or ground-glass opacities. No pneumothorax. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and Hila:
A new mildly enlarged right paratracheal lymph node at the 4R station now measures approximately 1.1 cm in short axis, where no pathologically enlarged node had been identified at the 12-month restaging. This represents new nodal disease in a station that had previously shown a complete radiographic response to osi. Right hilar nodes remain small and within normal limits. No new left-sided or subcarinal adenopathy. No anterior mediastinal masses. Esophagus is unremarkable.

Heart and Great Vessels:
Heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. Thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. No evidence of pulmonary embolism on this non-dedicated chest CT.

Chest Wall and Pleura:
No chest wall invasion. No pleural nodularity. No pleural thickening beyond what was previously characterized.

Upper Abdomen (imaged on chest protocol):
Liver: No hepatic lesions. No biliary ductal dilatation.
Spleen: Normal size and attenuation.
Adrenal Glands: Both adrenal glands are normal. No adrenal masses or nodules.
Upper pole kidneys: Unremarkable bilaterally.

Bony Structures:
Ventral thoracic vertebral bodies, ribs, clavicles, and scapulae are without suspicious lytic or blastic lesions. No pathologic fractures identified.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. NEW INTRACRANIAL METASTASES -- Three new enhancing brain lesions are identified, all new since the staging MRI brain performed 14 months prior. The lesions are most consistent with metastatic disease given the patient's known history of EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung carcinoma and the typical gray-white junction distribution. Specifically: (a) a 12 mm left frontal lobe lesion with moderate surrounding vasogenic edema and mild focal mass effect; (b) an 8 mm right cerebellar hemisphere lesion without significant edema; and (c) a 6 mm left parietal lobe lesion. The left frontal lesion is the dominant lesion and is the most likely contributor to the patient's current symptoms of morning headache and word-finding difficulty given proximity to the language network.

2. NO LEPTOMENINGEAL DISEASE -- No abnormal leptomeningeal enhancement is identified on this examination. The ventricular system is normal and there is no hydrocephalus.

3. SYSTEMIC DISEASE PROGRESSION IN CHEST -- Unambiguous interval enlargement of the right upper lobe primary/residual mass from 1.8 cm to 2.7 cm over approximately 2 months, following a prolonged plateau of partial response. This increase is well beyond the range of measurement variability and is consistent with progressive disease. Additionally, a new right paratracheal (4R) lymph node measuring 1.1 cm represents new regional nodal disease in a station with prior complete response.

4. NO NEW EXTRATHORACIC DISEASE -- No hepatic, adrenal, or visualized osseous lesions to suggest new distant metastatic disease sites beyond the intracranial findings.

Taken in totality, these findings are consistent with multifocal disease progression following prolonged response to osi, with development of both intracranial metastases and enlargement of the primary thoracic lesion with new regional lymphadenopathy. Clinical correlation with neurological examination and oncologic management team is appropriate. Neurosurgery and radiation oncology input may be warranted given the intracranial findings.

Electronically signed by:
Attending Radiologist, Neuroradiology and Thoracic Imaging
Department of Radiology
